chunk_summary: Pharmacotherapy for pediatric obesity is an adjunct to lifestyle treatment, recommended for adolescents ≥12 years with severe obesity or comorbidities. Baseline assessments, monitoring, and stopping rules are essential for safe and effective treatment.
learning_objectives: Understand eligibility criteria for pharmacotherapy in pediatric obesity management.
Identify baseline assessments required before initiating pharmacotherapy.
Recognize the importance of monitoring and stopping rules for pharmacotherapy.
Evaluate the role of pharmacotherapy as an adjunct to lifestyle interventions.
key_facts: Pharmacotherapy is recommended for adolescents ≥12 years with class 2 obesity plus life-threatening comorbidities or class 3 obesity (IAP, 2023).
Baseline assessments include BMI severity, comorbidities, medications, contraindications, and labs (e.g., BP, glucose, HbA1c, lipids, ALT).
Mental health screening (e.g., depression, suicidality, disordered eating) is critical before prescribing.
Post-menarcheal females require pregnancy risk assessment and counseling.
Pharmacotherapy is deemed ineffective if BMI does not reduce by ~5% after 12 weeks of consistent use (IAP, 2023).
Follow-up is recommended at 4–6 weeks for tolerability and adherence, and by 12 weeks for response.
reference_quote: The 2023 IAP obesity guideline recommends adjunct pharmacotherapy in adolescents aged 12 years or older with class 2 obesity plus immediate or life-threatening comorbidities, or class 3 obesity with or without comorbidities.
document_key_topics: Monitoring and stopping rules for obesity treatment
Adjunct role of pharmacotherapy with lifestyle interventions
